include::./_includes/attributes.adoc[]

= Migrate to Roq using an LLM

// Generated by Claude Code

Roq uses https://quarkus.io/guides/qute[Qute templates] and a different content model than Jekyll, Hugo, or other static site generators.
An LLM (Large Language Model) such as Claude, ChatGPT, or a locally hosted model can accelerate the migration of your templates, layouts, and content files.

This guide covers the recommended prompts and workflow for using an LLM to convert a Jekyll-based site to Roq.
The same approach works for Hugo, Eleventy, or other generators with minor prompt adjustments.

NOTE: LLM-assisted migration is not fully automatic. Expect to review and adjust the output. The prompts in this guide are a starting point -- refine them as you learn what works for your site.

== Prerequisites

* A working Roq project (see the https://docs.quarkiverse.io/quarkus-roq/dev/index.html[Getting Started guide])
* An existing static site you want to migrate (Jekyll, Hugo, or similar)
* Access to an LLM that supports long context and file attachments (Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, or a local model)

[[overview]]
== Overview of the migration process

Migrating a static site to Roq involves converting three categories of files:

1. **Layouts and templates** -- Liquid/Jinja/Go templates to Qute templates
2. **Content files** -- Markdown or AsciiDoc with front matter adjustments
3. **Configuration** -- `_config.yml` or equivalent to `application.properties`

An LLM handles template conversion well because:

* Template languages are well-represented in training data
* Conversions are mostly mechanical (syntax mapping)
* The LLM can explain its changes, which helps you learn Qute

Content files typically need minimal changes -- mostly front matter adjustments.
Configuration requires manual work because Roq's configuration model differs significantly from Jekyll's.

[[prepare]]
== Prepare reference material for the LLM

Before you start prompting, gather reference files that the LLM can use as context.
Providing concrete examples of working Roq templates produces much better results than relying on the LLM's training data alone.

Collect these files from a working Roq project:

* A sample Qute layout (for example, `templates/layouts/default.html`)
* A sample content page with front matter (for example, a `.md` or `.adoc` file from `content/`)
* Your `application.properties` file
* The Roq documentation on Qute templates: https://docs.quarkiverse.io/quarkus-roq/dev/index.html

TIP: If your LLM supports file attachments, attach these files directly. Otherwise, paste them into the conversation as code blocks.

[[convert-layouts]]
== Convert layouts and templates

Layouts are the highest-value target for LLM conversion.
Jekyll uses Liquid templates; Hugo uses Go templates.
Roq uses Qute, which has a different syntax but similar concepts.

=== Key syntax differences (Jekyll Liquid to Qute)

[cols="1,1,1", options="header"]
|===
| Concept
| Jekyll (Liquid)
| Roq (Qute)

| Variable output
| `\{{ page.title }}`
| `\{page.title}`

| Conditional
| `{% if page.image %}...{% endif %}`
| `\{#if page.image}...\{/if}`

| Loop
| `{% for post in site.posts %}...{% endfor %}`
| `\{#for post in site.collections.get('posts')}...\{/for}`

| Include
| `{% include header.html %}`
| `\{#include header.html /}`

| Layout inheritance
| `layout: default` in front matter
| `layout: default` in front matter (same concept)

| Content insertion
| `\{{ content }}`
| `\{#insert /}`
|===

=== Suggested prompt for layout conversion

Use a prompt similar to this one, attaching your source layout and a working Roq layout as reference:

[source,text]
----
I am migrating a static website from Jekyll to Roq (a Quarkus-based static
site generator that uses Qute templates).

Convert the attached Jekyll Liquid layout to a Roq Qute template.

Key rules:
- Replace Liquid {{ variable }} with Qute {variable} syntax
- Replace {% if %} with {#if } ... {/if}
- Replace {% for item in collection %} with {#for item in collection} ... {/for}
- Replace {{ content }} with {#insert /}
- Replace {% include file.html %} with {#include file /}
- Site-level variables use the `site` object (e.g., site.title)
- Page-level variables use the `page` object (e.g., page.title)
- Collections are accessed via site.collections.get('name')
- Preserve all HTML structure and CSS classes unchanged

Here is my Jekyll layout:
[paste or attach your layout file]

Here is an example of a working Roq Qute layout for reference:
[paste or attach a working Roq layout]
----

=== Iterate on complex layouts

For layouts with many includes, navigation logic, or pagination, break the work into pieces:

1. Convert the main layout first (usually `default.html`)
2. Convert each include file separately
3. Convert pagination logic last (Roq pagination works differently from Jekyll)

After each conversion, test in dev mode:

[source,shell]
----
quarkus dev
----

Fix errors before moving to the next file.

[[convert-content]]
== Convert content files

Content files (blog posts, pages) usually need fewer changes than layouts.
The main adjustments are in the YAML front matter.

=== Front matter differences

[cols="1,1,1", options="header"]
|===
| Field
| Jekyll
| Roq

| Layout
| `layout: post`
| `layout: post` (same)

| Date
| `date: 2024-01-15`
| `date: 2024-01-15` (same)

| Permalink
| `permalink: /about/`
| Use file path or `page-aliases`

| Categories
| `categories: [blog, tech]`
| Use `tags` or directory-based collections

| Excerpt
| `excerpt: "..."`
| `description: "..."`
|===

=== Suggested prompt for content migration

[source,text]
----
I am migrating content files from Jekyll to Roq.

Convert the YAML front matter in these files to Roq format:
- Keep `layout`, `title`, `date`, and `author` fields unchanged
- Rename `excerpt` to `description`
- Convert `permalink: /path/` to `page-aliases: /path/` (Roq uses the
  file path as the URL by default; aliases handle old URLs)
- Remove `categories` (use directory structure or `tags` instead)
- Keep the body content unchanged (Markdown and AsciiDoc work as-is)

Here are my content files:
[paste or attach files]
----

TIP: For bulk content migration, ask the LLM to write a script that processes all files in a directory rather than converting files one at a time.

[[convert-config]]
== Convert site configuration

Site configuration does not map cleanly between generators.
Use the LLM to get a starting point, then adjust manually.

=== Suggested prompt for configuration

[source,text]
----
I am migrating from Jekyll to Roq. Convert my Jekyll _config.yml to Roq
application.properties format.

Key mappings:
- `title` → site.title property or set in a Qute data file
- `url` / `baseurl` → site.url in application.properties
- `permalink` → Roq uses file-path-based URLs by default
- `plugins` → Roq uses Quarkus extensions (Maven dependencies)
- `collections` → site.collections.<name>.layout in application.properties
- `exclude` → quarkus.roq.site.ignored-files

Here is my Jekyll _config.yml:
[paste or attach _config.yml]
----

[[tips]]
== Tips for effective LLM-assisted migration

Ask for explanations::
When the LLM converts a template, ask it to explain each change.
This helps you learn Qute syntax and catch incorrect conversions.

Provide error messages::
If a converted template produces an error in `quarkus dev`, paste the full error message back to the LLM and ask it to fix the issue.

Batch similar files::
Group similar templates or content files and convert them together.
The LLM produces more consistent output when it can see patterns across files.

Verify Qute syntax::
After each conversion, run `quarkus dev` to validate.
Qute provides clear error messages with line numbers that you can feed back to the LLM.

Use the type-safe features::
Qute supports type-safe templates.
After the initial migration works, ask the LLM to help you add type annotations for better IDE support and compile-time checking.

[[limitations]]
== Known limitations

* **Liquid filters**: Jekyll's Liquid filters (for example, `| date: "%B %d, %Y"`) do not have direct Qute equivalents. Qute uses method calls instead (for example, `\{post.date.format('MMMM dd, yyyy')}`). The LLM usually handles this well, but verify date formats.
* **Jekyll plugins**: Jekyll plugins have no Roq equivalent. If your site uses plugins for sitemap generation, SEO tags, or feeds, check the available https://docs.quarkiverse.io/quarkus-roq/dev/index.html[Roq plugins] for alternatives.
* **Sass/SCSS processing**: Jekyll processes Sass files automatically. With Roq, use a CSS build tool or pre-compiled CSS. The LLM cannot automate this part.
* **Data files**: Jekyll's `_data/` directory maps to Roq's data extension. The file format (YAML/JSON) stays the same, but the access syntax in templates changes.

[[next-steps]]
== What's next

* Read the https://docs.quarkiverse.io/quarkus-roq/dev/index.html[Roq documentation] for the full feature set
* Check the https://github.com/quarkiverse/quarkus-roq[Roq repository] for example sites
* If you develop migration scripts or improved prompts, consider contributing them back to the project (see https://github.com/quarkiverse/quarkus-roq/issues/780[issue #780])
